### Post by SeijiSensei on 2012-06-15
First, is your nameserver authoritative for your domain, or are the DNS records hosted at GoDaddy?  If the latter, then it doesn't matter what you put in your local DNS.

Next, SPF applies to the domain, not to hosts.  On my servers, it looks like this:

```

@     IN    SOA    example.com. root.example.com.  (
                   [stuff]
                   )

      IN    NS     ns1.example.com.
      IN    NS     ns2.example.com.

      IN    MX     0 mail.example.com.
      IN    MX     5 mail2.example.com.

      IN    TXT    "v=spf1 a:smtp.example.com a:smtp2.example.com a:mail.example.com ~all"

[rest of the zone file]

```

That says that smtp, smtp2, and mail are all valid SMTP senders for example.com.

### Post by SeijiSensei on 2012-06-15
> @       IN      TXT     "v=spf1 a:mail.mysite ~all"  

I think you're missing the point.

The only server(s) that remote SMTP hosts will pay attention to are the ones at GoDaddy.  You need to add the record there.  The record you apparently entered at GoDaddy doesn't include a sending server name or address:

> "v=spf1 ~all"

Your mail server needs to have a public IP address with a corresponding A record at GoDaddy.  If you're on a residential connection without a static IP address, this is pretty hard to implement.  You'll have to update your records at GoDaddy each time your server's public address changes.

If you know your server's public address (probably the address assigned to your router by your ISP), then your GoDaddy records should look something like this:

```

@       IN    SOA  example.com. root.example.com. (
[stuff] 

        IN    TXT  "v=spf1 a:mail.example.com ~all"

mail    IN    A    10.10.10.10

```

where 10.10.10.10 is the public address used by your mail server.

### Post by SeijiSensei on 2012-06-15
```
@    3600    IN    TXT    "v=spf1 a:mail.mysite.org ~all"

```

That says mail.mysite.org is the legitimate sender for your domain.  The A record for that host points to "myexternalip".  If that is the address by which your mail server is visible to the outside world, then you're all set.

### Post by SeijiSensei on 2012-06-15
Perhaps because your IP address resolves back to rrcs-24-172-204-102.central.biz.rr.com rather than to mail.mysite.org.

Try replacing "a:mail.mysite.org" with "ip4:24.172.204.102". (Hint: You can't obfuscate your address if it's embedded in the reverse DNS hostname!)

If this is just a hobby project, then you can fiddle with your current configuration.  If this is an actual business project, you should make your forward and reverse DNS resolution be consistent. You might be able to get RoadRunner to change its reverse DNS to point to mail.mysite.org since you appear to have a business account with them. If not, you might consider investing in a virtual machine from a provider like [Linode]("http://www.linode.com/").  For $20/month you'll get a virtual server with a static IP address that you can point to mail.mysite.org.  Mail servers generally need to have consistent forward and reverse resolution in order to avoid anti-spam rules.
